task: I need to send a quick note to my supervisor about a schedule change that came up, and I want it to be easy to understand. Could you help me put it together so it gets the point across cleanly?

prefs.short: Prefers plain, respectful talk over polished formality
prefs.explanation: He comes across as practical and unpretentious, so overly formal language would likely feel stiff. A respectful, straightforward tone fits his outlook better than anything flashy or corporate. He seems more comfortable with everyday language than with performative politeness.

prefs.short: Wants answers that stay concise and to the point
prefs.explanation: His life is built around getting work done efficiently, so he likely values responses that do not ramble. Short, useful answers would fit his practical style better than long explanations. He probably only wants more detail when it directly helps him solve something.

He seems to prefer straightforward communication that gets to the point without extra hedging. His practical mindset suggests he would rather hear what matters than wade through softened language. Directness would likely feel more honest and useful to him.

prefs.short: Best served with simple bullets or checklists
prefs.explanation: He likely benefits from information that is easy to scan and act on quickly. Bullets or short checklists would match his work-focused, hands-on style better than dense prose. He seems like someone who would appreciate a clear sequence of steps when needed.

prefs.short: Prefers plain English, not technical wording
prefs.explanation: He does not seem to want jargon or fancy phrasing when asking for help. Plain English would fit his practical, grounded way of dealing with problems. If the wording gets too technical, it would probably feel unnecessary or tiring to him.

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here’s a clean, simple version:

“Hi [Supervisor Name], a schedule change came up, and I wanted to let you know as soon as possible. I’m handling it now and will keep you updated if anything changes.”

If you want, I can make it:
- more firm
- more casual
- or shorter, like a one-liner

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful language like "Hi [Supervisor Name]" and "I wanted to let you know as soon as possible," which feels straightforward rather than overly formal.
Keeps it concise: a short note plus brief options, with no rambling or unnecessary detail.
Direct and no-nonsense: "a schedule change came up" and "I’m handling it now" gets straight to the point without hedging.
The response is easy to scan and includes bullets for alternatives, though the main note itself is a short paragraph rather than a checklist.
Uses plain English and avoids jargon or technical wording; phrases like "schedule change" and "keep you updated" are simple and accessible.

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here’s a plain 2-line version:

“Hi [Supervisor Name], my schedule changed unexpectedly, so I wanted to let you know right away. I’m taking care of it and will keep you updated.”

If you want, I can make it even more specific to what changed.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful talk: "Hi [Supervisor Name]" and "wanted to let you know right away" are straightforward and unflashy.
Very concise: "here’s a plain 2-line version" and the note itself is short and to the point.
Clear and no-nonsense: "my schedule changed unexpectedly" and "I’m taking care of it" state the issue directly without hedging.
Not bullets/checklists, but it is easy to scan and act on; the response explicitly offers a "plain 2-line version" and keeps the message in a simple format.
Uses plain English with no jargon or technical wording; phrases like "schedule changed unexpectedly" and "keep you updated" are simple and accessible.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a plain 2-line version with the actual change included:

“Hi [Supervisor Name], my shift on [day] changed to [new time/location], so I wanted to let you know right away. I’m handling it now and will keep you updated.”

If you want, I can make it sound a little more natural or more supervisor-like.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful talk: "Hi [Supervisor Name]" and "wanted to let you know right away" are straightforward and unflashy.
Very concise: it explicitly says "here’s a plain 2-line version" and provides a short note with no rambling.
Clear and no-nonsense: "my shift on [day] changed to [new time/location]" states the issue directly, with no hedging.
Uses a simple, easy-to-scan format: "here’s a plain 2-line version" followed by a short quoted message.
Plain English throughout, with no jargon or technical wording; it stays everyday and easy to understand.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a plain 2-line version:

“Hi [Supervisor Name], my shift on Friday changed to 10 a.m. at the downtown office, so I wanted to let you know right away. I’m handling it now and will keep you updated.”

If you want, I can make it sound a little more work-like or a little shorter.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful language like "Hi [Supervisor Name]" and "wanted to let you know right away," avoiding stiff formality.
Very concise: "here’s a plain 2-line version" and the note itself is short and to the point.
Clear and direct, e.g. "my shift on Friday changed... so I wanted to let you know right away" with no hedging.
Provides a simple, easy-to-scan two-line message, which matches the checklist/bullets preference in spirit by keeping it structured and quick to read.
Uses plain English and avoids jargon or technical wording; phrases like "shift changed" and "keep you updated" are straightforward.
